minerals.visual_notes: Defining characteristic is banded or patterned appearance — concentric, plume, fortification, or other structured color patterns. The concentric fortification pattern in cross-section looks like aerial topography or castle walls.
prompt_fragments: concentric fortification banding in cross-section
earth-strata geologic calendar nodule
layered silica archive in waxy translucent bands
minerals.vibe_notes: Agate is a mineral calendar — each band a record of deposition. In cross-section it reads like geologic stratigraphy compressed into a hand-held nodule. The fortification pattern is one of the most compelling natural geometries.
minerals.folklore_summary: The name 'agate' is ancient, recorded by Theophrastus around 315 BCE. Believed to protect against storms, calm thirst, and bring luck in Greek and Roman traditions.

minerals.visual_notes: Alexandrite is unique — a single stone with two identities. Under northern daylight or fluorescent light it is distinctly green; under incandescent (candle, tungsten) light it turns vivid red-purple. The best Russian specimens shift from 'emerald' to 'ruby' — an impossible color change nicknamed 'the emerald by day, ruby by night' gem. No other natural gemstone produces such a dramatic and complete color change.
prompt_fragments: alexandrite: green under daylight, turning deep red-purple under incandescent bulb
Ural Mountains alexandrite — Tsar's gem shifting colors like Imperial Russia's uniform
emerald by day, ruby by night — the chromium paradox of chrysoberyl
minerals.vibe_notes: Alexandrite is the great paradox of gemology — one stone, two completely different identities, perfectly opposed. Green by day, red by night. The Russian gem named for a Tsar it would outlast by centuries. No other gem changes so completely.
symbolism: good luck and balanced opposing forces
minerals.folklore_summary: Alexandrite was officially discovered on April 17, 1834 — the 16th birthday of Crown Prince Alexander II of Russia, who would later become Tsar. The stone was named in his honor by mineralogist Nils Gustaf Nordenskiöld. Its green-and-red color change matched the military colors of Imperial Russia, m

minerals.visual_notes: Amber's most famous feature is inclusion preservation — ancient insects, spiders, plant fragments, feathers, and even vertebrate hair and skin have been preserved in extraordinary detail. The warm resinous golden color and organic warmth are unmatched by any mineral. Dominican blue amber appears an iridescent blue-green in reflected light — among the most extraordinary natural optical effects.
prompt_fragments: Baltic honey-amber — 50-million-year-old sunlight in polymerized resin
Burmese amber with Cretaceous insect inclusion — life suspended in ancient gold
Dominican blue amber glowing cerulean under fluorescent light
minerals.vibe_notes: Amber is fossilized time — sunlight from a forest that no longer exists, trapped in a matrix that still holds a 100-million-year-old insect at your fingertips. It is the original electrical material, the original trade gem, and one of the most emotionally warm stones in the collection.
minerals.folklore_summary: Amber has been traded over long distances for at least 13,000 years — Baltic amber has been found in Egyptian and Mycenaean archaeological sites thousands of kilometers from its origin. The Greek myth explained amber as the tears of the Heliades (sisters of Phaëton) who were transformed into poplar

minerals.visual_notes: Amethyst geodes from Brazil and Uruguay are among the most recognizable mineral display specimens globally. Hollow cavite filled with violet crystals pointing inward. The finest deep Siberian-grade material shows a red flash under incandescent light. Color zoning in angular patterns is characteristic and diagnostic.
prompt_fragments: amethyst geode cross-section — violet crystal cathedral in basalt egg
deep Siberian amethyst with red flash under incandescent light
Dionysus purple: iron-radiation alchemy crystallized in quartz
minerals.vibe_notes: Amethyst is the purple of wine, prayer, and power — used by bishops, kings, and Dionysus worshippers alike. A geode of amethyst is a cathedral floor inside a stone egg: thousands of violet crystals pointing inward at nothing and everything.
minerals.folklore_summary: The name 'amethyst' comes from Greek 'amethystos' (not intoxicated). Greek myth: Dionysus, god of wine, pursued a maiden named Amethystos who prayed to the goddess Artemis for protection; Artemis turned her to white quartz. Dionysus, repentant, poured wine over the crystal — turning it purple. Ancie

minerals.visual_notes: Aquamarine is the sea captured in hexagonal beryl — pale to deep blue with the clarity of tropical water. The Dom Pedro aquamarine (Smithsonian, carved by Bernd Munsteiner) is the largest faceted aquamarine in the world (10,363 ct). Long prismatic crystal form with striated faces is characteristic of large rough specimens.
prompt_fragments: aquamarine hexagonal prism — tropical sea-blue iron beryl from Brazil
Santa Maria deep-blue: compressed Atlantic light in beryllium silicate
sailor's gemstone — blue water clarity in Mohs-8 crystal
minerals.vibe_notes: Aquamarine is the color of shallow tropical sea over white sand — pale to deep blue-green, vitreous clear, the hexagonal form of ocean. The Dom Pedro obelisk in the Smithsonian is an 18-inch tower of the stuff. Sailors carried it for calm and protection. It still has that quality: cool, honest, clarifying.
minerals.folklore_summary: Ancient mariners believed aquamarine was the sacred gemstone of mermaids, cast up from the sea floor. Carrying aquamarine on a sea voyage was thought to bring calm seas, prevent seasickness, and protect against drowning. In Roman tradition it was sacred to Neptune. The association with water, clarit

minerals.visual_notes: Among the most intensely colored blue minerals in existence. The deep azure color is immediately striking. Azurmalachite — the intergrowth with green malachite — produces some of the most visually dramatic natural patterns in mineralogy.
prompt_fragments: deep azure radiating rosettes on copper-stained gossan matrix
azurmalachite swirl — blue fire meeting green venom
medieval painter's pigment ground from vivid copper-heaven blue
minerals.vibe_notes: Azurite is blue fire from copper chemistry — secondary, unstable, perpetually converting to malachite. The medieval painters who ground it to pigment knew it would fade and green. That fragility is part of its identity.
minerals.folklore_summary: In medieval Europe, azurite was called 'azzurrum' and was ground to produce blue paint; it was one of the few ways to achieve true blue before synthetic pigments. Many old paintings now show greenish skies where azurite has converted to malachite due to moisture exposure over centuries. Ancient Egyp

minerals.visual_notes: Hexagonal cross-section is diagnostic in hand specimens. Prismatic crystals can be enormous in pegmatites (multi-kilogram). Emerald is densely included compared to other varieties. Color zoning (core-to-rim or sector) occurs in some crystals.
prompt_fragments: hexagonal beryl prism in forest green
aquamarine sea-glass clarity
morganite rose-peach pegmatite gem
emerald jardin vivid green
minerals.vibe_notes: Beryl is a color-variety powerhouse — a single species that spans the gem rainbow from green to blue to pink to gold, all from trace chromophore swaps in the same hexagonal prism lattice.
minerals.folklore_summary: Ancient legend held that wearing an emerald could reveal truth — placing it under the tongue would supposedly make a person speak truthfully. Aquamarine was believed to be the treasure of mermaids.

minerals.visual_notes: Calcite's extraordinary morphological diversity makes it among the most recognizable minerals despite no single defining habit. Clear Iceland spar shows remarkable double refraction — images appear doubled. Dogtooth clusters are among the most architecturally beautiful mineral specimens.
prompt_fragments: dogtooth scalenohedral calcite — pale gold clusters on dark matrix
Iceland spar rhombohedron doubling every letter beneath it
cave stalactite drip-built calcite — slow geometry
mangano-calcite pink in hydrothermal veins
minerals.vibe_notes: Calcite is the mineral that built civilization's monuments and floors its caves with crystal cathedrals. It reacts with acid — a rare dramatic chemistry demonstration in geology. The most morphologically diverse mineral on Earth; no two habits look alike.
minerals.folklore_summary: Iceland spar's double refraction fascinated early scientists — it was central to Huygens' (1690) wave theory of light and was used in early polarimetry. Some researchers have proposed Viking navigators used Iceland spar to locate the sun in cloudy Arctic skies, though evidence remains debated. Calci

minerals.visual_notes: Madagascar celestite geodes — hollow limestone nodules lined with pale-blue tabular crystals — are among the most widely sold mineral specimens globally. The celestial blue color is gentle and consistent. Crystals often have a translucent quality with pearly luster on cleavage surfaces.
prompt_fragments: Madagascar celestite geode — hollow limestone cave of sky-blue tabular crystals
pale celestial-blue orthorhombic clusters — heaven's palette in strontium sulfate
pearly-blue celestite tabular crystal with pearlescent cleavage
minerals.vibe_notes: Celestite holds the color of open sky inside a limestone cave. The name is fitting — pale blue, pearly, translucent, quiet. Madagascar geodes are small sky-caverns. A gentle mineral with a celestial aspiration.
minerals.folklore_summary: The name 'celestite' or 'celestine' comes from Latin 'caelestis' (heavenly) — a reference to the pale sky-blue color. The element strontium was first isolated from celestite and strontianite found near Strontian, a village in Argyll, Scotland — giving the element its name. Madagascar's Sakoany celes

minerals.visual_notes: Characteristic waxy luster on polished surfaces. Translucent with a faint glow in thin sections. Smooth, ghost-like quality distinguishes it from coarse quartz.
prompt_fragments: waxy translucent microcrystalline silica
smooth ghost-white blue chalcedony surface
botryoidal cave-milk formations
minerals.vibe_notes: Chalcedony reads as quiet, cool, and internally lit. The waxy surface has a specific tactile quality — smooth and almost skin-like. The blue form evokes fog, still water, and negative space.
minerals.folklore_summary: Carnelian (a chalcedony variety) was one of the most important gem materials of the ancient world, used in amulets, seals, and jewelry across Egypt, Mesopotamia, and Rome.

minerals.visual_notes: The vivid cyan to turquoise-blue color is unmistakable. Chrysocolla is often the most visually striking mineral in copper oxidation zones. It frequently occurs with malachite (green), azurite (deep blue), and cuprite (red), creating spectacular polychrome specimens.
prompt_fragments: vivid cyan chrysocolla crust on copper ore
earthy blue-green mineraloid in oxidation zone
gem-silica translucent aqua cabochon
copper patina mineral decay blue
minerals.vibe_notes: Chrysocolla is the color of patinated copper — a vivid, slightly decayed cyan that evokes wet tropical ruins and ocean-floor chemistry. Its softness and mineraloid nature give it an impermanence that contrasts with its brilliant color.
minerals.folklore_summary: The ancient Greek name means 'gold solder' — reflecting its actual historical use as a flux material in gold soldering. In modern crystal markets it is often conflated with turquoise due to color similarity.

minerals.visual_notes: Citrine spans from pale lemon-yellow to deep Madeira orange-cognac. The warm golden tones made it popular in Art Deco jewelry. Large clean specimens are readily available and affordable. Heat-treated material has uniform color; natural citrine often has patchy or pale color with visible phantoms.
prompt_fragments: Madeira citrine: cognac-amber hexagonal quartz — warm iron-glow
golden citrine faceted cushion — Art Deco sunshine in quartz
November amber warmth in SiO2 — heated amethyst becomes solar
minerals.vibe_notes: Citrine is bottled autumn sunlight — the yellow-to-amber warmth of October afternoons in crystalline quartz. Madeira citrine burns like cognac held up to candlelight. The Art Deco period knew what to do with it.
minerals.folklore_summary: Citrine has been confused with topaz throughout most of gem history — 'Spanish topaz,' 'Brazilian topaz,' and 'Madeira topaz' were all actually citrine. The confusion was only definitively resolved in the early 20th century. In contemporary crystal culture, citrine is heavily associated with prosper

minerals.visual_notes: Unmistakable copper-red when fresh. Slow oxidation spectrum from red through brown and black to vivid blue-green patina is one of nature's most beautiful chemical sequences. Keweenaw Peninsula specimens show exceptional arborescent habits.
prompt_fragments: copper-red arborescent mass with blue-green malachite bloom
wiry dendritic copper on basalt matrix
warm metallic red fading to verdigris teal
aesthetic_roles: tonal contrast with blue-green secondary minerals
minerals.vibe_notes: Copper is the first chapter of human metallurgy — warm, workable, transformative. Its slow oxidation into blues and greens is one of nature's most beautiful chemical reactions.
minerals.folklore_summary: The word 'copper' derives from Latin 'cuprum', itself from Greek 'Kuprios' meaning 'of Cyprus', where copper was extensively mined in antiquity. Copper was the first metal systematically worked by humans, defining the Chalcolithic period. The Lake Superior copper deposits in Michigan supported one o

minerals.visual_notes: Gem-quality crystals prized as ruby and sapphire. Non-gem material is gray-brown and often massive. Silk inclusions produce asterism in star rubies and sapphires.
prompt_fragments: barrel-shaped trigonal corundum crystals
chromium fire of ruby red
midnight blue of sapphire iron-titanium charge transfer
six-rayed asterism from silk inclusions
minerals.vibe_notes: Corundum occupies the extreme end of mineral hardness and gem value. Ruby and sapphire represent fire and sky — the two poles of corundum's chromatic identity.
minerals.folklore_summary: Rubies were called 'ratnaraj' (king of gems) in Sanskrit texts. Sapphire was associated with divine favor in medieval European Christianity.

minerals.visual_notes: Unmistakable adamantine luster and exceptional brilliance in cut stones. Rough diamonds often appear greasy or frosted. Dispersion produces fire — flashes of spectral color — unmatched by any other colorless gemstone except moissanite.
prompt_fragments: octahedral rough diamond with adamantine luster and trigon etch pits
prism-clear light prison — cold fire and spectral flashes
kimberlite blue ground yielding crystallographic perfection
minerals.vibe_notes: Diamond is crystallographic perfection distilled from the Earth's mantle. It holds light like a prison — refracting, dispersing, returning it transformed. The hardest thing on Earth, yet cleavable with a single skilled blow.
minerals.folklore_summary: Diamond's name comes from Greek 'adamas' (unconquerable). Ancient Indians recognized diamond's hardness millennia before European contact. Hindu mythology associates vajra (diamond thunderbolt) with Indra's weapon. Medieval Europeans believed diamonds were tears of gods or splinters of stars. The De

minerals.visual_notes: Emerald's vivid green is the benchmark for lush saturated green in the gem world. The jardin (inclusion garden) is not a flaw in fine emerald — it is expected, accepted, and sometimes considered a signature of natural origin. The 'emerald cut' (rectangular step cut) was designed to showcase emerald's color while minimizing mechanical stress on the included stone.
prompt_fragments: Colombian emerald hexagonal prism — vivid chromium green with jardin inclusions
Muzo mine: hydrothermal green from black shale, spring-vivid Cr3+
Cleopatra's mine: 2000 years of green from Egypt's Eastern Desert
minerals.vibe_notes: Emerald is living green crystallized — the color of growth, deep jungle, and spring. Its jardin is not imperfection but biography — three-phase Colombian inclusions tell the story of the hydrothermal fluid that made it. The finest stones have a depth that no synthetic can replicate.
minerals.folklore_summary: Cleopatra's Mines in Egypt's Eastern Desert were the world's primary emerald source for Roman civilization. The Aztec god Quetzalcoatl was said to reside in emeralds; large emeralds were among the most sacred objects in pre-Columbian Mesoamerica — the Spanish conquest of Peru looted crystals the siz

minerals.visual_notes: Fire opal's vivid orange-to-red body color makes it the only facetable opal variety (when transparent). The color evokes live flame, molten metal, and sunset. In Mexico it is called 'piedra de fuego' (stone of fire) and was sacred to the Aztecs as a symbol of Huitzilopochtli.
prompt_fragments: transparent Mexican fire opal — concentrated sunlight in amorphous silica
Querétaro orange — volcanic glass holding warm flame
Aztec sacred fire-bird stone in vivid orange
minerals.vibe_notes: Fire opal is concentrated solar energy in amorphous silica — orange-red volcanic glass that holds the color of fire without being consumed by it. It has none of the spectral mystery of black opal; instead it confronts you with pure warm chromatic intensity.
minerals.folklore_summary: Aztec civilization held fire opal sacred — the 'stone of the fire bird' associated with the hummingbird avatar of Huitzilopochtli. The Spanish conquest brought fire opal to Europe, where it was considered an exotic curiosity but never achieved the commercial success of the later Australian market. M

minerals.visual_notes: One of the best minerals for geometric crystal logic and surreal color zoning.
prompt_fragments: translucent cubic crystal zoning
acid-violet fluorite geometry
UV-reactive candy mineral
minerals.vibe_notes: Great for apps that need cubes, zones, fluorescence, or impossible-looking candy color.

minerals.visual_notes: Unmistakable warm golden-yellow in fresh specimens. Arborescent and dendritic forms are among the most prized mineral specimens globally.
prompt_fragments: warm golden metallic luster
dendritic wire gold on white quartz matrix
nugget worn smooth by placer water
minerals.vibe_notes: Gold carries the weight of ten thousand years of human desire. It is simultaneously primordial and modern, terrestrial and cosmic.
minerals.folklore_summary: Gold has been considered sacred across virtually every ancient civilization. The alchemical pursuit of turning base metals into gold (chrysopoeia) dominated medieval science. Ancient Egyptians considered gold the flesh of the gods. In Hindu tradition, gold is associated with Lakshmi and prosperity.

minerals.visual_notes: Unmistakable greasy feel and extremely dark matte-black color. Marks paper and skin readily. Steel-gray metallic luster on fresh cleavage faces. Often found as disseminated flakes or foliated masses in metamorphic rocks.
prompt_fragments: foliated dark graphite plates with greasy metallic sheen
matte black scaly graphite in white marble matrix
carbon's humble terrestrial form — dark and slippery
minerals.vibe_notes: Graphite is diamond's humble alter ego — same element, opposite outcome. Where diamond captures and refracts, graphite absorbs and slides. It is carbon in its most pragmatic, terrestrial form.
minerals.folklore_summary: Graphite was initially called 'plumbago' (black lead) when discovered in Borrowdale in the 1560s — mistaken for a form of lead. The Borrowdale deposit was so pure and massive it could be sawn into sticks directly for writing, and so valuable that miners were searched leaving the mines. Graphite and

minerals.visual_notes: One of the most visually variable oxides. Specular hematite is mirror-silver; earthy hematite is brick-red. Red streak unites all forms. Iron roses are among the most dramatic mineral specimens.
prompt_fragments: mirror-bright specular hematite plates
red ochre ancient pigment dust
botryoidal kidney ore cross-section
iron rose rosette from Alpine fissure
minerals.vibe_notes: Hematite bridges the prehistoric and the industrial — the first pigment and a global iron ore. Red streak as diagnostic clue is an unforgettable mineralogical moment.
minerals.folklore_summary: Name from Greek 'haima' (blood) for the red streak and powder. Ancient amulets believed to stop bleeding. Ochre use predates all other known pigments.

minerals.visual_notes: Jadeite's waxy polish and semi-transparency in the finest green material ('glassy' or 'icy') is visually unmistakable. The fei-cui terminology in Chinese trade describes a full quality spectrum. A water-drop test (drops stand high on polished surface) and the cold, greasy feel are tactile identifiers used by experienced traders.
prompt_fragments: imperial jade vivid green semi-transparent glow
waxy jadeite polish cool to touch
subduction-zone mineral carved to perfection
lavender jadeite whisper of violet
minerals.vibe_notes: Jadeite carries perhaps the heaviest cultural weight of any mineral on Earth — the civilizational stone of China and Mesoamerica simultaneously, formed in the crushing pressure of subduction zones and revered for millennia. Imperial jade's vivid semi-transparent green is one of the most coveted colors in human history.
minerals.folklore_summary: In ancient China, the deceased were sometimes buried with jade pieces placed on bodily orifices to preserve the body — jade burial suits (玉衣) of the Han dynasty, made from thousands of jade plaques sewn with gold, silver, or bronze wire, represent the apex of this tradition. Jade was believed to pre

minerals.visual_notes: Opaque throughout — this is the key visual identifier. Takes a good polish showing vivid matte-to-waxy colors. Picture jasper is among the most visually complex natural stone materials.
prompt_fragments: opaque iron-oxide-stained microcrystalline silica
canyon-floor earth-body matte mineral
picture jasper landscape imagery in stone
minerals.vibe_notes: Jasper is the most opaque and earthy of the silica minerals. It reads as matter, ground, oxidized earth — the base layer beneath everything. Its matte warmth is fundamentally different from the translucent glow of chalcedony.
minerals.folklore_summary: One of the oldest named gemstones; 'jasper' appears in texts from ancient Babylonia, Egypt, and the Hebrew Bible. Red jasper was associated with blood and warrior energy across many ancient cultures.

minerals.visual_notes: The labradorescence appears as a floating, depth-inhabiting iridescence that shifts with viewing angle and light direction. Unlike surface iridescence, it appears to come from within the stone. The dark gray to near-black base color intensifies the color display. Best specimens show full-spectrum color shift across a single face.
prompt_fragments: floating prismatic aurora iridescence in dark gray feldspar
thin-film interference labradorescence shifting blue-green-gold
northern lights imprisoned in polished stone
minerals.vibe_notes: Labradorite carries its color inside — it is not a surface phenomenon but a depth effect. The dark ground color against the shifting iridescence creates a sense of something luminous moving beneath a dark surface. Spectrolite, with its near-black matrix and full-spectrum display, is one of the most visually complex single mineral specimens on earth.
minerals.folklore_summary: Inuit legend describes the aurora borealis as frozen light trapped inside labradorite. Moravian missionaries encountering the stone in Labrador in 1770 were reportedly struck by the play of color. The Finnish spectrolite variety was not discovered until World War II when a German bomb crater reveale

minerals.visual_notes: Lepidolite is one of the most visually distinctive micas due to its lavender/pink color — the only common lavender mica. In pegmatites, it often forms spectacular masses of pale lavender scales intergrown with pink tourmaline (rubellite) and rose quartz. The pearly luster on cleavage surfaces gives a soft, shimmering quality.
prompt_fragments: lavender scaly lepidolite mica in pegmatite
soft lilac pearly shimmer mineral
lithium-bearing pink mica books
lepidolite intergrown with pink tourmaline
minerals.vibe_notes: Lepidolite is the gentlest common pegmatite mineral — a pastel lavender mica shimmer against stark feldspar and quartz. Its lithium content gives it a surprising industrial dimension hidden beneath its soft aesthetic. In combination with pink tourmaline and rose quartz, it creates some of the most feminine-coded pegmatite assemblages.
minerals.folklore_summary: The lavender color and lithium-bearing nature have combined in popular imagination to make lepidolite a stone of 'calm and peace' in New Age traditions. The real lithium connection — as one of the oldest lithium mineral sources — gives it an unexpected industrial modernity beneath its gentle pastel

minerals.visual_notes: One of the strongest 'ornamental pattern' minerals in existence.
prompt_fragments: botryoidal copper-green domes
banded malachite concentric eyes
ornamental toxic-jewel swirl pattern
minerals.vibe_notes: Perfect for anything ornate, hypnotic, green, dangerous, or ceremonial.

minerals.visual_notes: Adularescence appears as a floating, glowing light phenomenon that rolls or shifts as the stone is moved — unlike surface iridescence, it appears to emanate from within the stone. The finest blue moonstone from Sri Lanka has an electric, almost liquid blue light that rolls across the interior.
prompt_fragments: floating blue light billowing under translucent feldspar surface
lunar milk adularescence in near-colorless K-feldspar cabochon
night-water cool glow of Sri Lankan blue moonstone
minerals.vibe_notes: Moonstone has one of the most evocative optical effects in mineralogy — the adularescence gives it a liquid, otherworldly quality as if light is floating just beneath the surface. The blue variety in particular has an electric, cold, lunar quality. It is the mineral most consistently associated with water, night, and the feminine across unconnected cultures.
minerals.folklore_summary: Pliny the Elder wrote that moonstone's appearance changed with the phases of the moon. Roman legend held that it was formed from moonlight solidified. In India, moonstone is considered a sacred stone and a dream stone — sleeping with it is said to bring beautiful visions. The Art Nouveau jewelers of

minerals.visual_notes: Morganite's soft rose-peach color is inherently gentle and warm — less assertive than ruby, less cold than pink sapphire. Large clean crystals are available. The rose-gold + morganite combination became dominant in contemporary jewelry design in the 2010s.
prompt_fragments: morganite: peach-rose beryl from Cs-Li pegmatite — warm manganese hexagonal form
Madagascar morganite tabular crystal — blush warmth in beryllium silicate
rose-gold + morganite: 2010s design archetype in soft pink beryl
minerals.vibe_notes: Morganite is the warm, quiet sister of emerald and aquamarine — the peachy pink end of beryl's spectrum. It lacks the drama of other beryls but has a consistent warmth and gentleness. The rose-gold era made it a design archetype.
minerals.folklore_summary: Morganite is a relatively recently named gem variety (1911) compared to emerald and aquamarine. George Frederick Kunz named it for J.P. Morgan, one of the great mineral collectors of the Gilded Age — whose collections are now at the American Museum of Natural History and Harvard's Mineralogical Muse

minerals.visual_notes: Nephrite's waxy sheen and subtle translucency are distinctive. White mutton-fat jade has a soft luminosity unlike any other stone. The green varieties can look similar to jadeite but typically show a more subdued, less intense green. The fibrous microstructure is visible under magnification as a tangled felt-like mass.
prompt_fragments: waxy spinach-green nephrite with fibrous felt texture
mutton-fat white jade soft luminosity
pounamu greenstone sacred Pacific stone
ancient jade carving millennia of tradition
minerals.vibe_notes: Nephrite is the older jade — the stone that launched millennia of Chinese and Pacific jade culture before jadeite arrived. Its mutton-fat white and spinach green are among the most culturally loaded color-material combinations in human history. It is not the most beautiful stone, but it may be the most meaningful.
minerals.folklore_summary: In Chinese tradition, jade is said to have a 'living' quality — it absorbs the essence of its wearer over time and gives back healing energy. It is said to feel alive in a way that other stones do not. A jade bangle that breaks is said to have 'sacrificed itself' to protect its owner from harm.

minerals.visual_notes: Obsidian is pure volcanic glass — the most visually simple and the most dramatically effective black mineral material. Its jet-black vitreous surface reflects light cleanly. Rainbow varieties produce full spectral color from thin-film magnetite layers. Apache tears appear opaque but transmit brownish light when held up.
prompt_fragments: jet-black volcanic glass with conchoidal fracture — razor edge from deep time
rainbow obsidian — full spectral iridescence on absolute black surface
Aztec smoking mirror: Tezcatlipoca sees all in volcanic glass
minerals.vibe_notes: Obsidian is the void made solid — a black mirror that reflects what you put in front of it. It is simultaneously the sharpest natural material and the darkest. Tezcatlipoca's smoking mirror sees all. Apache tears carry grief transformed into translucent darkness.
minerals.folklore_summary: Tezcatlipoca, the Aztec god of night, sorcery, and the north, is symbolized by a black obsidian smoking mirror — his attribute and the means by which he could see all things in the universe including human sin and fate. Obsidian mirrors were used by priests for scrying and divination. The Apache tea

minerals.visual_notes: Precious opal's play-of-color is unlike any other optical effect in the mineral world — full spectrum visible in shifting patches as the viewing angle changes. The diffraction grating created by ordered spheres splits white light into all visible wavelengths simultaneously.
prompt_fragments: black opal from Lightning Ridge — volcanic spectral fire on dark sea
white precious opal with rolling green-blue spectral patches
boulder opal in ironstone matrix — veins of spectral light
minerals.vibe_notes: Opal is the mineral of all light at once. Where diamond bends light and ruby absorbs it, opal diffracts it — splitting white into every visible wavelength simultaneously across a surface that seems to hold living fire inside it.
minerals.folklore_summary: Opal has the most dramatic reputation reversal in gem history. Ancient Romans paid extravagant sums — Pliny wrote that a Roman senator was exiled rather than sell his opal to Mark Antony. Medieval tradition held opal lucky — a stone containing all gem colors in one. Sir Walter Scott's 1829 novel Ann

minerals.visual_notes: The preserved wood grain and annual rings are the defining visual feature — the stone tells a biological story. Cross-sections are among the most visually striking mineral-art objects, showing perfect tree anatomy in glowing chalcedony. Color zonation often follows the original wood structure.
prompt_fragments: petrified wood cross-section showing ancient tree rings in chalcedony
rust-red Triassic log silicified in Arizona desert
agatized wood banded multicolor fossil
deep time mineral wood grain preserved in stone
minerals.vibe_notes: Petrified wood is the most legible deep-time object — a tree that became a stone, grain by grain, over millions of years, while preserving every ring and vessel. It is the bridge between biology and mineralogy, and one of the most emotionally resonant materials in the natural world.
minerals.folklore_summary: Many Indigenous traditions of the American Southwest regard petrified wood as sacred — the bones of ancient beings or the material remains of primordial forests. The Navajo creation narrative describes a primordial world with vast forests; the scattered logs of the Petrified Forest are explained in

minerals.visual_notes: Pyrite is mineralogy's great impersonator — 'fool's gold' for millennia. The striated faces of pyrite cubes (from oscillation between cube and pyritohedron) are diagnostic. Iron cross twins — two interlocking pyritohedra — are among mineralogy's most architecturally perfect specimens. Pyrite suns (flat radiating discs) are unique and immediately recognizable forms.
prompt_fragments: perfect Navajún pyrite cube — striated brassy faces of absolute geometric order
iron cross twin: two pyritohedra intersecting at 90° — mineralogy's signature form
pyrite sun: flat radiating disc of brassy crystals from Illinois coal seam
minerals.vibe_notes: Pyrite is the great deceiver — the mineral that convinced explorers they'd found gold and sent fleets home to disgrace. But a perfect Navajún pyrite cube is not deception — it is geometric perfection in the service of iron sulfide chemistry. Iron cross twins are architecture. Pyrite suns are geology's mandalas.
minerals.folklore_summary: Pyrite has been 'fool's gold' since antiquity — its brass-yellow metallic luster has deceived prospectors, miners, and explorers throughout history. Captain Martin Frobisher returned from his 1576 Arctic expedition with 200 tonnes of 'ore' that turned out to be pyrite — the English Crown had funded

minerals.visual_notes: One of the most visually flexible minerals on earth.
prompt_fragments: glassy trigonal crystal logic
clear silica prisms
drusy quartz glitter
minerals.vibe_notes: Useful as a baseline mineral, a structure mineral, and a palette-shifting species.

minerals.visual_notes: The banded 'Inca Rose' stalactitic form from Argentina — polished to reveal concentric pink-and-white rings — is one of the most recognized ornamental stone patterns in the gem world. Crystallized raspberry-red specimens from Colorado are among the most sought-after collector mineral specimens globally.
prompt_fragments: raspberry-red curved rhombohedral crystals on white quartz matrix
Inca Rose banded stalactite — concentric pink and cream rings
warm rose-manganese architecture of Sweet Home Mine
minerals.vibe_notes: Rhodochrosite lives between pink and red — the color of living tissue, roses, and sunsets. The Inca Rose banding reads as geological diary — each layer a year of cave drip. The Sweet Home crystals are raspberry-red architectural perfection.
minerals.folklore_summary: Argentine tradition holds that rhodochrosite (Inca Rose) was formed from the blood of ancient Inca kings and queens crystallized over time — giving the stone its name 'Rosa del Inca' (Rose of the Inca). Argentina declared it the national gemstone in 2002. The Sweet Home Mine in Colorado produced spe

minerals.visual_notes: Rose quartz has a characteristic soft, diffuse, milky-pink quality from the light-scattering fibrous inclusions. It is never facet-grade transparent in classic form. The color is gentle and warm — not vivid like ruby or pink tourmaline but consistently soft. Star rose quartz cut as cabochon shows the six-rayed star most vividly under a point light source.
prompt_fragments: rose quartz massive — diffuse pink warmth in translucent quartz fog
star rose quartz cabochon — six-rayed asterism in milky blush
Venus stone: Brazil pegmatite pink dispersed through massive silica
minerals.vibe_notes: Rose quartz is mineral warmth — not vivid, not sharp, not crystalline. It diffuses its pink into the whole mass and softens light into rose. It is the texture of a gentle thing, the color of trust and care.
minerals.folklore_summary: Rose quartz has one of the most robust modern metaphysical followings of any mineral. Ancient Assyrian and Roman cosmetic recipes include rose quartz facial rollers — a practice that has seen a massive modern revival. Egyptian legend held that rose quartz was a gift from Aphrodite (via Eros) to huma

minerals.visual_notes: Ruby is the red variety of corundum — the intensity of Cr3+ fluorescence in daylight gives Burmese rubies an inner glow that red garnets and spinel cannot replicate. In strong sunlight, a fine Burmese ruby appears to emit light. The six-rayed star in star ruby is one of the most captivating optical effects in gemology.
prompt_fragments: pigeon's blood ruby — chromium fire glowing in Mogok marble
star ruby cabochon with six-rayed silk asterism
vivid red fluorescent corundum — blood of gems
minerals.vibe_notes: Ruby holds the deep-red that human eyes are tuned to desire — the color of blood, roses, and fire condensed into Mohs-9 corundum. In strong daylight, Burmese pigeon's blood ruby appears to glow from within — the chromium fluorescence adding spectral energy that no mere pigment can produce.
minerals.folklore_summary: Ruby is the 'king of gems' — a title given in Sanskrit texts that has persisted across cultures. Burmese warriors inserted rubies under their skin before battle believing it would make them invincible. Medieval European tradition held that a ruby's color deepened to warn its wearer of approaching da

minerals.visual_notes: Kashmir sapphire produces a 'velvety' blue that seems to glow from within — caused by fine silk inclusions scattering light. Star sapphire at its best shows a perfectly centered six-rayed star that moves over the cabochon surface. Padparadscha's salmon-pink-orange is unique and defies category — it must be evaluated in person to appreciate.
prompt_fragments: velvety Kashmir blue — cornflower sky trapped in Mohs-9 corundum
star sapphire cabochon — six-rayed asterism rolling over blue-gray surface
padparadscha: salmon-lotus at the boundary of pink and orange, Sri Lanka
minerals.vibe_notes: Sapphire is the blue of the sky trapped in stone — or in Persian belief, the reason the sky is blue. Kashmir sapphire has the quality of depth without darkness, blue without coldness, velvet without softness. The finest stones seem to emit blue light rather than reflect it.
minerals.folklore_summary: Ancient Persians believed the sky was blue because the Earth rested on a vast sapphire pedestal whose reflection made the sky blue. Medieval European clergy wore sapphire rings as stones of heaven. The name comes from Greek 'sappheiros' (possibly originally referring to lapis lazuli, not sapphire).

minerals.visual_notes: Fresh specimens show brilliant silver-white metallic luster. Tarnish develops complex gray-black patinas. Kongsberg wire-silver specimens are among the most celebrated native metal mineral specimens in existence.
prompt_fragments: twisted wire silver on black matrix
brilliant silver-white metallic gleam fading to tarnished gray
arborescent silver branches like frozen lightning
minerals.vibe_notes: Silver is gold's lunar twin — cooler, more mysterious, marked by its tendency to darken and tarnish over time into complex gray-black patinas.
minerals.folklore_summary: Silver has been mined since at least 3000 BCE in Anatolia. The Laurion mines near Athens funded Athenian naval power. Kongsberg, Norway produced extraordinary arborescent silver specimens prized by collectors since the 17th century. In mythology, silver is the metal of the moon — opposite gold's sol

minerals.visual_notes: Smithsonite is primarily known as a secondary zinc carbonate with exceptional color range and unusual pearlescent botryoidal habit. Cobalt-bearing blue smithsonite from Tsumeb and copper-bearing green from Arizona are collector favorites. The pearly luster of botryoidal forms is distinctive.
prompt_fragments: pearlescent botryoidal smithsonite domes — periwinkle blue cobalt
apple-green cuprian smithsonite over gossan matrix
secondary zinc carbonate translucent skin on limestone
minerals.vibe_notes: Smithsonite is quiet but extraordinary — a carbonate that refracts light through translucent botryoidal skins in a dozen colors. The cobalt-blue variety from Tsumeb has a quality like polished sky.
minerals.folklore_summary: Smithsonite is named for James Smithson — chemist, mineralogist, and benefactor of the Smithsonian Institution. He actually studied calamine (a historical term covering hemimorphite and smithsonite). The mineral was formally named for him posthumously by François Sulpice Beudant in 1832.

minerals.visual_notes: Smoky quartz produces the most varied depth range of any colored quartz — from barely-there smoke-gray to absolute black. Large faceted smoky quartz gems (hundreds to thousands of carats) are obtainable and affordable. The phantoms (ghost crystal growth zones) visible inside larger crystals add visual complexity.
prompt_fragments: large smoky quartz prism — alpine gray crystal from radiation alchemy
morion quartz near-black opacity — shadow crystallized
phantom smoky quartz: clear-to-dark growth zones visible in transparent gray
minerals.vibe_notes: Smoky quartz is where quartz goes dark — radiation absorbed in aluminum defects, the landscape of granite made visible in crystal. Phantoms within large stones show the history of growth: clear to smoky to clearer again, time made visible.
minerals.folklore_summary: Cairngorm quartz from the Cairngorm Mountains of Scotland is an iconic national gemstone tradition — large brown-gray crystals were set in the handles of Highland dirks, kilt pins, and traditional brooches. Victorian mourning jewelry used dark stones including morion (black smoky quartz) alongside j

minerals.visual_notes: Vivid sulfur-yellow color is highly diagnostic. Fine bipyramidal crystals from Sicily are among the most visually distinctive native element specimens. Surface luster characteristically resinous to waxy.
prompt_fragments: vivid sulfur-yellow bipyramidal crystals on white matrix
volcanic fumarole encrustation in acid yellow and orange
glowing brimstone — ancient and alien
minerals.vibe_notes: Sulfur smells like the beginning and the end of the world. At Kawah Ijen, miners carry glowing blue flames of burning sulfur on their backs in the dark — one of Earth's most alien industrial scenes.
minerals.folklore_summary: Sulfur (brimstone) is one of the oldest known mineral substances — referenced in ancient Vedic texts, Homeric epics, and the Bible. The biblical 'fire and brimstone' likely references volcanic sulfur. Alchemy designated sulfur as one of the three primes representing the soul. Kawah Ijen, Indonesia,

minerals.visual_notes: Aventurescence in sunstone appears as a glittering, spangling metallic flash — brighter and more dramatic than the soft glow of adularescence in moonstone. Oregon sunstone is the only known feldspar with native copper inclusions, giving it a uniquely warm, saturated color. The metallic sparkle in Norwegian sunstone has a coppery-gold quality.
prompt_fragments: warm copper-orange metallic glitter in transparent feldspar
solar fragment scattered-light aventurescence
Oregon native copper feldspar with deep bicolor red-green pleochroism
minerals.vibe_notes: Sunstone has a grounded, warm, solar quality that contrasts with the cool lunar depth of moonstone. The aventurescent glitter reads as scattered light, like sunlight on moving water or the scatter of a copper fire. Oregon sunstone, with its native copper and rich saturated color, is among the most texturally and chromatically complex feldspars.
minerals.folklore_summary: The term 'sólarsteinn' (sunstone) from Norse sagas refers to a navigational crystal, which has generated scholarly debate. Most evidence points to calcite (Iceland spar) or cordierite as the actual navigational stone, not feldspar sunstone — the overlap of the trade name with the saga reference is a

minerals.visual_notes: Chatoyancy (cat's eye effect) is the defining visual feature. The golden-brown silky shimmer moves across the surface as the viewing angle shifts. The parallel fiber orientation is always visible in cross-section.
prompt_fragments: golden silky chatoyant cat's eye effect in amber-brown fibrous silica
predator-eye desert surveillance mineral with shimmering fiber bands
pseudomorph after crocidolite with iron oxide golden shimmer
minerals.vibe_notes: Tiger's eye has a quality of focused, watchful warmth — it looks like something is looking back. The chatoyancy gives it movement and life. The amber-gold color range places it in hot, dry, predatory aesthetic territory.
minerals.folklore_summary: The cat's eye effect has long been associated with the protective gaze of predatory animals. Tiger's eye has been used as a protective talisman against the evil eye in various traditions.

minerals.visual_notes: The striated prism faces running parallel to the long axis (c-axis) are diagnostic in hand specimens. Cross-sections showing rounded triangular outline are highly characteristic. Watermelon tourmaline slices are immediately recognizable. Schorl is ubiquitous as jet-black prismatic crystals in pegmatites globally.
prompt_fragments: striated black schorl prism in pegmatite
neon Paraíba blue electric tourmaline
watermelon tourmaline cross-section pink and green
rubellite hot-pink columnar crystal
minerals.vibe_notes: Tourmaline is the most compositionally diverse gem mineral group on Earth — it can be literally any color, making it a universal chromatic reference. Watermelon tourmaline cross-sections are one of the most visually iconic mineral forms. Paraíba's neon glow is among the most intense colors in the gem world.
minerals.folklore_summary: Tourmaline was called the 'electric stone' after its pyroelectric properties were described by scientists in the 18th century. It was used to clean tobacco pipes by Dutch sailors because of its ash-attracting properties when heated.

minerals.visual_notes: Turquoise is one of the most culturally loaded minerals in human history. The blue that defined a color name ('turquoise'), ancient trade routes, and sacred objects across the Old and New Worlds. Matrix spiderweb patterns are prized for their aesthetic complexity — black or brown veins threading through blue-green backgrounds.
prompt_fragments: robin's-egg-blue waxy surface with dark matrix spiderweb veining
ancient sky-stone — desert blue carved into Aztec death mask mosaic
Persian turquoise: sky condensed into stone, no matrix, pure sky-blue
minerals.vibe_notes: Turquoise is the sky-stone of human history — found at the oldest mines, in the most sacred grave goods, across the most distant trading cultures. It is simultaneously the color of desert sky and deep sea, of protective amulets and ceremonial power.
minerals.folklore_summary: Turquoise is one of the few gem minerals whose name gave a color its name in European languages ('turquoise' from 'Turkish stone' — via the Turkish trade routes through which Persian turquoise reached Europe). Ancient Persians believed turquoise reflected the color of heaven. In Mesoamerica, turquoi
